docs.title: Run / Deployment checklist
docs.text:
1. Create virtualenv and install requirements.
  2. Set environment variables (ELECTRUM RPC credentials, DJANGO secret, Celery broker).
  3. Run python manage.py migrate.
  4. Start Electrum daemon on the same host and ensure RPC is enabled and wallet is loaded.
  5. Start Redis.
  6. Start Celery worker: celery -A simple_django_login_and_register worker --loglevel=info.
  7. Start Celery beat: celery -A simple_django_login_and_register beat --loglevel=info.
  8. Serve Django with Gunicorn/ASGI behind Nginx.

docs.title: Security notes
docs.text:
• Keep Electrum RPC bound to localhost or internal network; use firewall rules.
  • Use strong RPC credentials and store them in secrets manager (not checked into git).
  • Consider using SSL/TLS or SSH tunnel if RPC access crosses machines.

docs.title: What I didn't include (you can add)
docs.text:
• Email notifications / admin UI
  • Webhooks from Electrum (example uses polling)
  • Rate-limiting / concurrency safeguards when multiple payments come in
  • Unit tests and error metrics

If you want, I can now generate the exact files for you to copy — or create a downloadable zip. What would you like next?

docs.title: Serve qr codes dynamically
docs.text:
Ah! That 404 tells us exactly why the QR code isn’t showing: your template is trying to load a static PNG file at /qr_codes/1.png, but Django can’t find it. Django doesn’t automatically serve files from arbitrary folders, you need to either use static files correctly or generate the QR code dynamically.

Here’s how to fix it:

docs.title: Option 1: Serve QR codes dynamically (recommended)
docs.text:
Instead of saving 1.png on disk, generate the QR code in your view and return it dynamically. Example:

# views.py
import qrcode
from io import BytesIO
from django.http import HttpResponse

def qr_code_image(request, id):
    btc_address = "your_btc_address_here"  # or get it based on `id`

    qr = qrcode.QRCode(version=1, box_size=10, border=5)
    qr.add_data(btc_address)
    qr.make(fit=True)
    img = qr.make_image(fill='black', back_color='white')

    buffer = BytesIO()
    img.save(buffer, format="PNG")
    buffer.seek(0)

    return HttpResponse(buffer, content_type="image/png")

Add a URL pattern:

# urls.py
from django.urls import path
from . import views

urlpatterns = [
    path('qr_codes/<int:id>.png', views.qr_code_image, name='qr_code_image'),
]

Then in your template:

<img src="{% url 'qr_code_image' id=1 %}" alt="BTC QR code">

✅ This avoids saving files on disk and works for any QR code on demand.

docs.title: Option 2: Serve QR codes as static files
docs.text:
1. Put your PNG files in a folder, e.g. static/qr_codes/1.png
  2. Make sure Django knows your static folder:
# settings.py
STATIC_URL = '/static/'
  1. In template, reference the QR code:
{% load static %}
<img src="{% static 'qr_codes/1.png' %}" alt="BTC QR code">

The dynamic method (Option 1) is preferred for BTC payments because you usually generate unique QR codes for each transaction.

If you want, I can rewrite your /investments/create/ view to generate QR codes dynamically for each investment so they show correctly without 404s.

Do you want me to do that?

docs.title: Connect to MySQL Database with PDO
docs.text:
To connect to a MySQL database using PHP's PDO (PHP Data Objects), you need to follow these steps. PDO is a flexible and secure way to interact with databases and is highly recommended for use in modern PHP applications.

Here’s a basic example of how to connect to a MySQL database using PDO:

docs.title: Steps to Connect to MySQL Database with PDO:
1. Basic PDO Connection Example:
docs.text:
<?php
$host = '127.0.0.1'; // or 'localhost' or the IP address of the MySQL server
$db = 'dashboard'; // Database name
$user = 'jenkins'; // MySQL username
$pass = 'Zaichik1.&'; // MySQL password
$charset = 'utf8mb4'; // Charset for proper encoding

$dsn = "mysql:host=$host;dbname=$db;charset=$charset"; // Data Source Name

$options = [
    PDO::ATTR_ERRMODE            => PDO::ERRMODE_EXCEPTION, // Throw exceptions on errors
    PDO::ATTR_DEFAULT_FETCH_MODE => PDO::FETCH_ASSOC, // Fetch data as associative arrays
    PDO::ATTR_EMULATE_PREPARES   => false, // Disable emulation of prepared statements
];

try {
    $pdo = new PDO($dsn, $user, $pass, $options); // Create PDO instance
    echo "Connected to the database successfully!";
} catch (PDOException $e) {
    echo 'Connection failed: ' . $e->getMessage();
}
?>

docs.title: 2. Explanation of the Code:
docs.text:
• $host: This is the hostname or IP address of your MySQL server. If you are using Docker, and MySQL is in a different container, replace '127.0.0.1' with the service name of the MySQL container from the docker-compose.yml file (e.g., mysql).
  • $db: The name of the database you're connecting to (in your case, dashboard).
  • $user and $pass: Credentials for connecting to MySQL.
  • $charset: Setting the character set to ensure proper encoding, especially for special characters.
  • $dsn: Data Source Name string, used by PDO to define the connection type, host, and database.
  • $options: Additional options to make the PDO connection more secure and easier to handle errors.

docs.title: 3. Using PDO in a Docker Environment:
docs.text:
In a Docker environment, if you're trying to connect to a MySQL container from a PHP container (e.g., in your docker-compose.yml setup), you need to ensure the host is the service name of the MySQL container. For your setup, it would likely look like this:

<?php
$host = 'mysql'; // Service name from docker-compose.yml

So your connection string might look like this:

<?php
$host = 'mysql'; // Service name from docker-compose.yml
$db = 'dashboard';
$user = 'jenkins';
$pass = 'Zaichik1.&';
$charset = 'utf8mb4';

$dsn = "mysql:host=$host;dbname=$db;charset=$charset";

$options = [
    PDO::ATTR_ERRMODE            => PDO::ERRMODE_EXCEPTION,
    PDO::ATTR_DEFAULT_FETCH_MODE => PDO::FETCH_ASSOC,
    PDO::ATTR_EMULATE_PREPARES   => false,
];

try {
    $pdo = new PDO($dsn, $user, $pass, $options);
    echo "Connected to the database successfully!";
} catch (PDOException $e) {
    echo 'Connection failed: ' . $e->getMessage();
}

docs.title: 4. Testing the Connection:
docs.text:
To test the connection, you can create a simple PHP script using the above code, save it as test_pdo.php, and run it in your web server or PHP environment (within your Docker container).

  • Place this script in the root of your application (/application in your setup).
  • Access it via your browser or by using docker exec to run the script inside the PHP container:
docker exec -it <php-fpm-container-name> php /application/test_pdo.php

docs.title: 5. Troubleshooting:
docs.text:
• Database connection errors: If the connection fails, the error message returned by $e->getMessage() will help debug the issue.
  • Container hostnames: Ensure that you're using the correct service name (e.g., mysql) as the host if you're running MySQL inside a Docker container.
  • Ports: Make sure the correct MySQL port (3306) is exposed in the docker-compose.yml and is accessible.

docs.title: Next Steps:
docs.text:
• Once you're connected, you can run queries using PDO’s prepare, execute, and fetch methods for safe and efficient database interaction.

Let me know if you need further clarification!

docs.title: Copy Docker image to other host
docs.text: To copy a Docker image from one host to another, you can follow these steps. The general process involves saving the Docker image as a .tar file, transferring it to the other host, and then loading the image on the destination host.

docs.title: 1. Save the Docker Image as a .tar File:
docs.text:
On the source host, save the Docker image to a file using the docker save command.

docker save -o my_image.tar <image_name>

For example: 

docker save -o my_image.tar my_app:latest

This command creates a file called my_image.tar containing the specified Docker image.

docs.title: 2. Transfer the Image to the Destination Host:
docs.text:
Now, transfer the my_image.tar file to the destination host. You can use tools like scp or rsync for this:

  • Using scp: 
    scp my_image.tar user@destination_host:/path/to/destination/

Example: 

scp my_image.tar user@192.168.1.100:/home/user/

  • Using rsync: 
    rsync -avz my_image.tar user@destination_host:/path/to/destination/

Alternatively, you can use other file transfer methods such as SFTP, FTP, or even a USB drive if needed.

docs.title: 3. Load the Docker Image on the Destination Host:
docs.text:
Once the .tar file is transferred to the destination host, load it into Docker with the docker load command.

docker load -i /path/to/my_image.tar

For example: 

docker load -i /home/user/my_image.tar

This will import the image into Docker on the destination host. You can then verify that the image has been loaded by running:

docker images

docs.title: 4. (Optional) Tag the Image:
docs.text:
If needed, you can retag the image on the destination host using the docker tag command:

docker tag <image_id> new_tag_name:latest

For example: 

docker tag my_app:latest my_new_app:latest

docs.title: Summary:
docs.text:
1. Save the image on the source host: 
    docker save -o my_image.tar my_app:latest
  2. Transfer the .tar file to the destination host using scp, rsync, or another method.
  3. Load the image on the destination host: 
    docker load -i /path/to/my_image.tar
  4. (Optional) Retag the image if necessary.

Once done, the Docker image will be available on the other host.

docs.title: Docker create image from running container
docs.text:
To create a Docker image from a running container, you can use the docker commit command. This allows you to capture the current state of a running container and save it as a new image.

Here’s a step-by-step guide on how to create an image from a running Docker container:

docs.title: Steps:
docs.text:
1. List Running Containers: First, identify the container you want to create an image from.
docker ps

This will show a list of running containers along with their container IDs and names. Note the container ID or name of the container you want to create an image from.

  1. Commit the Running Container to an Image: Use the docker commit command to create an image from the running container.
docker commit <container_id_or_name> <repository_name>:<tag>
  • <container_id_or_name>: The ID or name of the container you want to commit.
  • <repository_name>: The name you want to give the new image.
  • <tag>: Optional tag to give the image (e.g., v1, latest). If omitted, Docker will use latest by default.

Example: 

docker commit abc12345 my_custom_image:latest

This creates an image called my_custom_image with the tag latest from the container with the ID abc12345.

  1. List Docker Images: Once the image has been created, you can confirm it by listing all images.
docker images

You should see your newly created image in the list.

docs.title: Example of Committing a Container to an Image:
docs.text:
# List running containers
docker ps

# Assuming you have a running container with ID abc12345
docker commit abc12345 my_custom_image:latest

# Verify that the image was created
docker images

docs.title: Optional: Adding a Commit Message
docs.text:
You can also add a commit message to describe the changes you made:

docker commit -m "Added custom configurations" abc12345 my_custom_image:latest

docs.title: Optional: Pausing the Container
docs.text:
While this is optional, some users prefer to pause the container before committing it to ensure that no changes are made while creating the image.

docker pause <container_id_or_name>
docker commit <container_id_or_name> <repository_name>:<tag>
docker unpause <container_id_or_name>

docs.title: Using the Newly Created Image:
docs.text:
After creating the image, you can start a new container from it using docker run:

docker run -d my_custom_image:latest

docs.title: Summary:
docs.text:
• Use docker commit to create an image from a running container.
  • Specify the container ID and the name of the new image.
  • Optionally, add tags and commit messages to describe the changes.

docs.title: Import data in docker MySQL
docs.text: To import data into a MySQL database running inside a Docker container, you can follow these steps. You can either import a SQL dump file during the container creation process or use a running container to import the data.

docs.title: 1. Import Data During Docker Container Initialization
docs.text:
You can place your SQL dump file (e.g., mydatabase_dump.sql) into a Docker volume or mount a directory from your host and use Docker's docker-entrypoint-initdb.d feature. MySQL will automatically import any SQL file placed in this directory when the container starts.

Here’s how to do it:

docs.title: Docker Compose Example:
docs.text:
Create a docker-compose.yml file:

version: '3.8'
services:
  mysql:
    image: mysql:8.0
    container_name: my_mysql
    environment:
      MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD: rootpassword
      MYSQL_DATABASE: mydatabase
      MYSQL_USER: myuser
      MYSQL_PASSWORD: mypassword
    volumes:
      - ./data:/var/lib/mysql  # Volume for MySQL data
      - ./init:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d  # Directory for initialization scripts
    ports:
      - "3306:3306"

Now, place your mydatabase_dump.sql file in the ./init/ directory (on your host system). When you start the container, MySQL will automatically import this SQL file.

docker-compose up

docs.title: 2. Import Data Into a Running MySQL Container
docs.text: If your MySQL container is already running, you can import a SQL file using the following steps:

docs.title: Step 1: Copy the SQL File Into the Container
docs.text:
You can use the docker cp command to copy the SQL dump file from the host system to the MySQL container.

docker cp mydatabase_dump.sql <container_name>:/mydatabase_dump.sql

For example:

docker cp mydatabase_dump.sql my_mysql:/mydatabase_dump.sql

docs.title: Step 2: Execute the mysql Command Inside the Container
docs.text:
Once the file is inside the container, you can use the mysql command to import the database.

docker exec -i <container_name> mysql -u <username> -p<password> <database_name> < /mydatabase_dump.sql

For example:

docker exec -i my_mysql mysql -u myuser -pmypassword mydatabase < /mydatabase_dump.sql

This command will import the SQL dump into the specified MySQL database.

docs.title: 3. Import Using a Volume Mount
docs.text:
You can also mount the SQL dump file directly when starting the container, and import the data using the same command:

docker run --name my_mysql -v /path/to/mydatabase_dump.sql:/mydatabase_dump.sql -e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=rootpassword -d mysql:8.0

Then, execute the import:

docker exec -i my_mysql mysql -u root -prootpassword mydatabase < /mydatabase_dump.sql

docs.title: Troubleshooting:
docs.text:
• Ensure the database (mydatabase) exists before importing. You can create it manually or via the MYSQL_DATABASE environment variable during container setup.
  • If you're importing large datasets, ensure you adjust MySQL’s settings (like max_allowed_packet).

docs.title: Remove all Docker images and containers
docs.text: To remove all Docker images and containers, you can follow these steps:

docs.title: 1. Stop and remove all containers:
docs.text:
First, stop all running containers, and then remove them.

# Stop all running containers
docker stop $(docker ps -q)

# Remove all containers
docker rm $(docker ps -a -q)

docs.title: 2. Remove all images:
docs.text:
After removing the containers, you can remove all Docker images.

# Remove all images
docker rmi $(docker images -q)

docs.title: 3. Optional: Remove all volumes (if needed):
docs.text:
If you also want to remove all Docker volumes, you can do that as well.

# Remove all volumes
docker volume rm $(docker volume ls -q)

docs.title: 4. Optional: Remove all networks (if needed):
docs.text:
You can also clean up all unused networks.

# Remove all networks
docker network rm $(docker network ls -q)

Be careful with these commands, as they will remove everything without confirmation.

docs.title: Cold electrum wallet
docs.text: Using a cold Electrum wallet ensures added security for your Bitcoin transactions by keeping your private keys offline. Here's a step-by-step guide on setting it up and using it to send and receive Bitcoin:

docs.title: Setup Cold Electrum Wallet
docs.text:
1. Install Electrum:
  2. Download the Electrum wallet from its official site: https://electrum.org.

  3. Install it on an offline computer that will act as your "cold" machine.

  4. Create a New Wallet:

  5. Open Electrum on the offline computer.
  6. Select Standard Wallet → Create a New Seed.

  7. Write down the seed phrase and store it securely offline.

  8. Verify Seed:

  9. Re-enter the seed phrase when prompted to confirm.

  10. Set a strong password for the wallet.

  11. Save Wallet File:

  12. Save the wallet file locally on your offline computer.

  13. Export the Watch-Only Wallet:

  14. Go to Wallet > Information to get the Master Public Key (xpub).
  15. Save the xpub key.
  16. Install Electrum on an online computer (this is your "online" wallet) and create a new wallet using the Use Public or Private Keys option. Paste the xpub key to create a watch-only wallet.

docs.title: Receiving Bitcoin
docs.text:
1. Get a Receiving Address:
  2. In your watch-only wallet (online), click Receive to generate a new receiving address.

  3. Share this address with the sender.

  4. Monitor Balance:

  5. You can see incoming transactions in your watch-only wallet. Your cold wallet remains offline and secure.

docs.title: Sending Bitcoin
docs.text:
1. Create a Transaction:
  2. In your watch-only wallet (online), go to Send.
  3. Enter the recipient's address and amount.

  4. Click Preview and save the unsigned transaction as a file (e.g., transaction.txn).

  5. Sign the Transaction Offline:

  6. Transfer the unsigned transaction file to the offline computer using a USB drive.
  7. Open Electrum on the cold wallet, go to Tools > Load Transaction > From File, and select the transaction file.

  8. Click Sign to sign the transaction, then save the signed file (e.g., signed_transaction.txn).

  9. Broadcast the Transaction:

  10. Transfer the signed transaction file back to the online computer.
  11. Open Electrum on the online machine, go to Tools > Load Transaction > From File, and load the signed transaction.
  12. Click Broadcast to send the transaction to the Bitcoin network.

docs.title: Important Security Tips
docs.text:
• Keep the Cold Wallet Offline: Ensure the cold wallet is never connected to the internet.
  • Secure Seed Phrase: Store the seed phrase in a safe location, such as a fireproof safe or secure storage.
  • Verify Downloads: Always verify the Electrum installer using the provided signatures.

By separating signing and broadcasting functions, the cold wallet ensures your private keys remain secure while facilitating Bitcoin transactions.

For more detailed Electrum wallet tutorials, visit the official Electrum documentation.

docs.title: Electrum CLI cheat sheet
docs.text: Perfect. Here’s a short, working Electrum CLI cheat sheet for the current versions (≥4.5), matching the style you’re running.

docs.title: 🔧 Daemon management
docs.text:
electrum daemon -d      # start daemon in background
electrum stop           # stop daemon
electrum getinfo        # show status of daemon

docs.title: 👛 Wallet management
docs.text:
electrum create         # create a new wallet (default location ~/.electrum/wallets/default_wallet)
electrum restore        # restore wallet from seed or master key
electrum load_wallet    # load a wallet into the daemon
electrum list_wallets   # list loaded wallets

docs.title: 💳 Address handling
docs.text:
electrum createnewaddress          # generate a new receiving address
electrum listaddresses             # list all addresses in the wallet
electrum listaddresses --funded    # list addresses with balance

docs.title: 💰 Balances
docs.text:
electrum getbalance      # show wallet balance (confirmed + unconfirmed)
electrum getbalance -a   # show balance of all addresses

docs.title: 📤 Transactions
docs.text:
electrum payto <address> <amount>    # create transaction
electrum broadcast <raw_tx>          # broadcast raw transaction
electrum history                     # show transaction history

docs.title: 🔑 Security
docs.text:
electrum password                  # change wallet password
electrum getprivatekeys <address>  # export private key(s) for an address

⚡️ Pro tip: If you want to script these in Django (via subprocess or JSON-RPC), the commands are the same — just remove interactive bits (like password prompts).

Do you want me to also give you the equivalent JSON-RPC calls (for each of these commands), so your Django app can talk directly to Electrum without subprocess.run()?

docs.title: Install elcectrum wallet
docs.text: To install the Electrum wallet, follow these steps:

docs.title: 1. Download Electrum
docs.text:
• Visit the official Electrum website: https://electrum.org.
  • Download the appropriate version for your operating system:
  • Windows: .exe file
  • MacOS: .dmg file
  • Linux: .tar.gz file or via package manager
  • Android: Available on the Google Play Store

docs.title: 2. Verify the Download
docs.text:
It's important to verify the authenticity of the Electrum installer to ensure it hasn't been tampered with. This step involves checking the signature provided on the website.

  • Download the signature file (.asc) from the site.
  • Use GPG to verify the signature: 
    gpg --verify electrum-x.x.x.exe.asc electrum-x.x.x.exe
  • If the verification succeeds, proceed with the installation.

docs.title: Windows:
docs.text:
• Double-click the downloaded .exe file and follow the installation prompts.
  • Optionally, select "Run as portable" during installation if you don't want to install it system-wide.

docs.title: MacOS:
docs.text:
• Open the .dmg file and drag the Electrum app to your Applications folder.
  • Open the app, and confirm it's from a trusted developer if macOS asks for confirmation.

docs.title: Linux:
docs.text:
• Extract the downloaded .tar.gz file: 
    tar -xvzf electrum-x.x.x.tar.gz
  • Navigate to the directory and run Electrum: 
    python3 electrum
    Alternatively, install via your package manager (though versions might not be the latest): 
    sudo apt install electrum  # For Debian/Ubuntu-based distros

docs.title: Android:
docs.text:
• Open the Google Play Store and search for Electrum Wallet.
  • Download and install the app.

docs.title: 4. Configure the Wallet
docs.text:
• Launch Electrum after installation.
  • Choose:
  • Create a new wallet: If this is your first wallet.
  • Restore a wallet: If you already have a recovery seed.
  • Follow the setup wizard to complete your wallet configuration.

docs.title: Tips for Security
docs.text:
• Always download Electrum from the official site.
  • Verify the download's integrity and authenticity.
  • Store your seed phrase securely and never share it.

For more details, visit the Electrum Documentation.

docs.title: Install and Configure Electrum with systemd and JSON-RPC
1. Install Electrum
docs.text: Official download: https://electrum.org/#download

docs.title: Install dependencies:
docs.text: sudo apt-get install python3-pyqt6 libsecp256k1-dev python3-cryptography

docs.title: Download release and signature:
docs.text:
wget https://download.electrum.org/4.6.2/Electrum-4.6.2.tar.gz
wget https://download.electrum.org/4.6.2/Electrum-4.6.2.tar.gz.asc

docs.title: Import developer GPG keys:
docs.text:
gpg --keyserver hkps://keys.openpgp.org --recv-keys 6694D8DE7BE8EE5631BED9502BD5824B7F9470E6

Alternative key servers:

gpg --keyserver keys.gnupg.net --recv-keys 6694D8DE7BE8EE5631BED9502BD5824B7F9470E6
gpg --keyserver hkps://keyserver.ubuntu.com --recv-keys 6694D8DE7BE8EE5631BED9502BD5824B7F9470E6
gpg --keyserver hkps://pgp.mit.edu --recv-keys 6694D8DE7BE8EE5631BED9502BD5824B7F9470E6

docs.title: Verify the signature:
docs.text:
gpg --verify Electrum-4.6.2.tar.gz.asc Electrum-4.6.2.tar.gz

Expected output if valid:

gpg: Good signature from "Thomas Voegtlin (https://electrum.org) <thomasv@electrum.org>"

If you see:

gpg: Can't check signature: No public key

Import more keys:

gpg --keyserver hkps://keys.openpgp.org --recv-keys \
    6694D8DE7BE8EE5631BED9502BD5824B7F9470E6 \
    637DB1E23370F84AFF88CCE03152347D07DA627C \
    AA0BC6824B397BBA99776E157ED8D82B37192688 \
    0EEDCFD5CAFB459067349B23CA9EEEC43DF911DC

docs.title: 3. Install Electrum in a Python Virtual Environment
System dependencies (outside venv):
docs.text:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y build-essential autoconf automake libtool pkg-config python3-dev libssl-dev

docs.title: Extract Electrum:
docs.text:
mkdir -p /var/www/simple-django-login-and-register/electrum
tar -xvf Electrum-4.6.2.tar.gz -C /var/www/simple-django-login-and-register/electrum

docs.title: Create and activate venv:
docs.text:
cd /var/www/simple-django-login-and-register
python3 -m venv ~/.venv
source ~/.venv/bin/activate

docs.title: Install Electrum:
docs.text:
cd /var/www/simple-django-login-and-register/electrum/Electrum-4.6.2
pip install -r electrum/Electrum-4.6.2/contrib/requirements/requirements.txt
pip install .

docs.title: Verify installation:
docs.text:
which electrum

Expected output:

/var/www/simple-django-login-and-register/.venv/bin/electrum

Check version:

electrum --version

docs.title: 4. Systemd Service for Electrum Daemon (JSON-RPC)
docs.text:
Create systemd service:

sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/electrum.service

Paste:

[Unit]
Description=Electrum Bitcoin Wallet Daemon
After=network.target

[Service]
User=al1
Group=al1
WorkingDirectory=/home/al1/.electrum

ExecStartPre=/bin/rm -f ~/.electrum/daemon.lock
ExecStartPre=/bin/rm -f ~/.electrum/wallets/*.lock

ExecStart=/var/www/simple-django-login-and-register/.venv/bin/electrum daemon -d

Restart=always
RestartSec=5

Environment=ELECTRUM_PATH=~/.electrum

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

docs.title: 5. Configure Electrum JSON-RPC
docs.text:
Edit config:

nano ~/.electrum/config

Example config:

{
  "auto_connect": true,
  "blockchain_preferred_block": {
    "hash": "000000000019d6689c085ae165831e934ff763ae46a2a6c172b3f1b60a8ce26f",
    "height": 0
  },
  "config_version": 3,
  "oneserver": true,
  "rpchost": "127.0.0.1",
  "rpcpassword": "supersecurepass",
  "rpcport": 7777,
  "rpcuser": "al1",
  "use_ssl": false
}

docs.title: 6. Manage Service
docs.text:
Reload systemd and start service:

sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl restart electrum
sudo systemctl enable --now electrum

Check status:

sudo systemctl status electrum

Expected:

● electrum.service - Electrum Bitcoin Wallet Daemon
     Loaded: loaded (/etc/systemd/system/electrum.service; enabled; preset: enabled)
     Active: active (running) since Tue 2025-09-09 21:54:18 BST; 13ms ago
    Process: 96235 ExecStartPre=/bin/rm -f /home/al1/.electrum/daemon.lock (code=exited, status=0/SUCCESS)
    Process: 96237 ExecStartPre=/bin/rm -f /home/al1/.electrum/wallets/*.lock (code=exited, status=0/SUCCESS)
   Main PID: 96239 (electrum)
      Tasks: 1 (limit: 18645)
     Memory: 3.1M (peak: 3.1M)
        CPU: 20ms
     CGroup: /system.slice/electrum.service
             └─96239 /var/www/simple/.venv/bin/python3 /var/www/simple/.venv/bin/electrum daemon -d

Sep 09 21:54:18 ku systemd[1]: Starting electrum.service - Electrum Bitcoin Wallet Daemon...
Sep 09 21:54:18 ku systemd[1]: Started electrum.service - Electrum Bitcoin Wallet Daemon.

docs.title: 7. Test JSON-RPC
docs.text:
Run:

/var/www/simple/.venv/bin/electrum getinfo

Example output:

{
    "auto_connect": false,
    "blockchain_height": 897119,
    "connected": false,
    "fee_estimates": {},
    "network": "mainnet",
    "path": "/home/al1/.electrum",
    "server": "localhost",
    "server_height": 0,
    "spv_nodes": 0,
    "version": "4.6.2"
}

Do you want me to also add curl JSON-RPC examples (like checking balance, creating invoices, sending transactions) so you can interact with Electrum programmatically?

docs.title: Install electrum cli
docs.text: There are two main ways to install Electrum, depending on what you need (desktop GUI vs CLI/daemon on a server). Since you’re working with a Django backend, I’ll assume you want the CLI/daemon version for server use.

docs.title: 1. Install from Python package (server/CLI use)
docs.text:
Electrum is a Python app. Best way is to install from source (not PyPI, because pip install electrum doesn’t work).

# Ensure build deps
sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y python3-pip python3-venv python3-dev build-essential \
    automake autoconf libtool pkg-config libssl-dev libffi-dev libsecp256k1-dev git

# Create a virtual environment (recommended)
mkdir -p ~/electrum && cd ~/electrum
python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate

# Clone Electrum source
git clone https://github.com/spesmilo/electrum.git
cd electrum

# Install requirements
pip install .[fast]

# Verify install
electrum --version

Now you can run:

electrum daemon start

docs.title: 2. Install official binary (desktop wallet with GUI)
docs.text:
If you want the desktop GUI version:

# Get latest release
wget https://download.electrum.org/4.6.1/Electrum-4.6.1.tar.gz
tar -xvf Electrum-4.6.1.tar.gz
cd Electrum-4.6.1
python3 run_electrum

Official binaries (signed by Thomas Voegtlin, Electrum’s maintainer) are at: 👉 https://electrum.org/#download

docs.title: 3. Run from AppImage (Linux desktop easiest)
docs.text:
wget https://download.electrum.org/4.6.1/electrum-4.6.1-x86_64.AppImage
chmod +x electrum-4.6.1-x86_64.AppImage
./electrum-4.6.1-x86_64.AppImage

✅ Summary for you (server + Django):

  • Use method 1 (from GitHub source) in a virtualenv.
  • That way you can run electrum daemon and talk to it via JSON-RPC.

Do you want me to give you a step-by-step for Ubuntu 22.04+ specifically (since some dependencies like autogen.sh caused you errors earlier)?

docs.title: JSONRPC interface
docs.text:
Commands to the Electrum daemon can be sent using JSONRPC. This is useful if you want to use electrum in a PHP script.

Note that the daemon uses a random port number by default. In order to use a stable port number, you need to set the 'rpcport' configuration variable (and to restart the daemon):

electrum setconfig rpcport 7777

Further, starting with Electrum 3.0.5, the JSON-RPC interface is authenticated using HTTP basic auth.

The username and the password are config variables. When first started, Electrum will initialise both; the password will be set to a random string. You can of course change them afterwards (the same way as the port, and then restart the daemon). To simply look up their value:

electrum getconfig rpcuser
electrum getconfig rpcpassword

Note that HTTP basic auth sends the username and the password unencrypted as part of the request. While using it on localhost is fine in our opinion, using it across an untrusted LAN or the Internet is not secure. Hence, you should take further measures in such cases, such as wrapping the connection in a secure tunnel. For further details, read this.

After setting a static port, and configuring authentication, we can perform queries using curl or PHP. Example:

curl --data-binary '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":"curltext","method":"getbalance","params":[]}' http://username:password@127.0.0.1:7777

Query with named parameters:

curl --data-binary '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":"curltext","method":"listaddresses","params":{"funded":true}}' http://username:password@127.0.0.1:7777

Create a payment request:

curl --data-binary '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":"curltext","method":"add_request","params":{"amount":"3.14","memo":"test"}}' http://username:password@127.0.0.1:7777

docs.title: JSON-RPC setup
docs.text: Alright — here’s the ready-to-use Electrum daemon JSON-RPC setup for your Django BTC payment listener. This will let your Django app call Electrum over HTTP and check balances, transaction status, etc., without running Bitcoin Core.

docs.title: 1. Create Electrum config file
docs.text:
Electrum stores its config in a directory like:

  • Linux: ~/.electrum/config
  • If you use -D flag, you can set a custom directory.

We’ll make one in your server’s home:

mkdir -p ~/.electrum
nano ~/.electrum/config

Paste this:

{
  "rpcuser": "btcadmin",
  "rpcpassword": "supersecurepass",
  "rpcport": 7777,
  "rpchost": "127.0.0.1",
  "use_ssl": false,
  "server": "electrum.blockstream.info:50002:s",
  "oneserver": true,
  "auto_connect": true
}

Explanation:

  • rpcuser / rpcpassword → Django will use this to authenticate.
  • rpcport → Default Electrum JSON-RPC port is 7777.
  • server → Using Blockstream’s Electrum server for mainnet.
  • oneserver + auto_connect → Makes connection stable for payments.

docs.title: 2. Start Electrum daemon with RPC
docs.text:
From your activated virtual environment:

electrum daemon -d -D ~/.electrum

Check:

electrum daemon status

You should see "daemon_status": "running".

docs.title: 3. Test RPC access from the server
docs.text:
curl --data '{"id":1,"method":"getbalance"}' \
     -u btcadmin:supersecurepass \
     http://127.0.0.1:7777

Expected output (example):

{"id":1, "result":{"confirmed": 0.0, "unconfirmed": 0.0}}

docs.title: 4. Use in Django
docs.text:
You can call Electrum directly from your views, Celery tasks, or a webhook listener:

import requests
from requests.auth import HTTPBasicAuth

RPC_URL = "http://127.0.0.1:7777"
RPC_USER = "btcadmin"
RPC_PASS = "supersecurepass"

def electrum_rpc(method, params=None):
    payload = {
        "id": 1,
        "method": method,
        "params": params or []
    }
    r = requests.post(RPC_URL, json=payload, auth=HTTPBasicAuth(RPC_USER, RPC_PASS))
    r.raise_for_status()
    return r.json()["result"]

# Example: get balance
balance = electrum_rpc("getbalance")
print(balance)

docs.title: 5. Auto-start on server boot
docs.text:
Create a systemd service so Electrum is always running:

sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/electrum.service

Paste:

[Unit]
Description=Electrum Bitcoin Wallet Daemon
After=network.target

[Service]
User=al
ExecStart=/var/www/simple-django-login-and-register/.venv/bin/electrum daemon -d -D /home/al/.electrum
Restart=always

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

Then:

sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable --now electrum

Now your Electrum RPC service will:

  • Start automatically on reboot
  • Be available to your Django backend
  • Use a trusted Electrum server (or you can point it to your own)

If you want, I can now give you a Django model + webhook listener that:

  • Generates a new BTC address for each user
  • Listens for incoming payments via Electrum RPC
  • Marks invoices as paid when confirmed

That would complete your vevrecapital.net BTC payment flow. Do you want me to prepare that next?

docs.title: Verify Git tag signatures
docs.text:
Great question — verifying Git tag signatures is a solid way to make sure you’re building Electrum (or any project) from an authentic source.

Here’s how to do it step by step:

docs.title: 1. Clone Electrum’s repo
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/spesmilo/electrum.git
cd electrum

docs.title: 2. Fetch all tags (signed release tags)
docs.text: git fetch --tags

docs.title: 3. Verify a release tag
docs.text:
For example, if you want to check 4.6.1:

git tag -v 4.6.1

You should see output like:

object 123abc...
type commit
tag 4.6.1
tagger Thomas Voegtlin <thomasv@electrum.org> 2024-10-01 12:34:56 +0000

Electrum 4.6.1 release
gpg: Signature made ...
gpg: Good signature from "Thomas Voegtlin (https://electrum.org) <thomasv@electrum.org>"

✅ Good signature means the tag is valid.

docs.title: 4. Import Thomas Voegtlin’s GPG key if needed
docs.text:
If you haven’t already:

gpg --keyserver hkps://keys.openpgp.org --recv-keys 6694D8DE7BE8EE5631BED9502BD5824B7F9470E6

docs.title: 5. Checkout the release
docs.text:
Once verified:

git checkout 4.6.1

Now you’re on the verified source code for Electrum 4.6.1.

⚡ This is better than just git clone and pip install . because you pin to a cryptographically signed release.

Do you want me to make you a one-liner shell script that clones Electrum, verifies the latest signed tag, and checks it out automatically?

docs.title: Verify the Electrum
docs.text:
Yes, Alex — you should verify the Electrum download, especially if you’re using it in production or handling customer funds.

Here’s why:

  • Electrum binaries are not distributed through PyPI, so you normally download them from electrum.org.
  • There have been real-world phishing attacks where fake Electrum installers tricked users into giving up their Bitcoin.
  • Verifying ensures the file wasn’t tampered with.

docs.title: 🔑 Verification steps (Linux example)
docs.text:
1. Download the files
wget https://download.electrum.org/4.6.1/Electrum-4.6.1.tar.gz
wget https://download.electrum.org/4.6.1/Electrum-4.6.1.tar.gz.asc
  1. Import the developer’s GPG key
gpg --keyserver hkps://keys.openpgp.org --recv-keys 6694D8DE7BE8EE5631BED9502BD5824B7F9470E6

(That’s Thomas Voegtlin’s signing key, Electrum’s lead dev.)

  1. Verify signature
gpg --verify Electrum-4.6.1.tar.gz.asc Electrum-4.6.1.tar.gz

✅ You should see:

gpg: Good signature from "Thomas Voegtlin (https://electrum.org) <thomasv@electrum.org>"

docs.title: If you’re installing via source (git clone):
docs.text:
• You don’t strictly need to verify if you trust GitHub directly, but in production, it’s best practice to either:

    • Verify Git tag signatures, or
    • Pin to a known commit hash.

⚠️ If you skip verification in a test/dev environment, it’s usually fine. But for production handling BTC, I’d strongly recommend verifying.

Do you want me to write you a ready-to-run Bash script that automates the Electrum download + GPG verification?

docs.title: C1-level exercises
docs.text: Absolutely, Alex! Here are some more C1-level exercises with answers for you to practice:

docs.title: 1. Complete the sentences with the correct form of the verb (use the correct tense and voice):
docs.text: a) By the time we arrived, the guests __________ (already / leave). b) If he __________ (study) harder, he would have passed the exam. c) The project __________ (complete) by the team before the deadline next week. d) She __________ (not / speak) to him since the argument last month. e) Had I known about the meeting, I __________ (attend).

docs.title: 2. Transform the sentences into reported speech:
docs.text: a) She said, "I will finish the report tomorrow." b) He asked, "Have you ever been to London?" c) They said, "We are going to start the project next week." d) She said, "I don't like spicy food." e) He asked, "Can you help me with this task?"

docs.title: 3. Use the correct modal verb to express the meaning:
docs.text: a) It is necessary to wear a uniform at work. b) There is a strong possibility that it will rain later. c) Giving advice politely to a friend. d) Talking about a hypothetical situation in the past. e) Expressing permission formally.

docs.title: 4. Fill in the blanks with appropriate linking words:
docs.text: a) I wanted to go to the party, __________ I was too tired. b) She passed the exam, __________ she didn’t study much. c) We will leave early, __________ the traffic is bad. d) He is very talented, __________ he doesn’t practice enough. e) I’ll call you __________ I arrive home.

docs.title: 1. Verb forms
docs.text: a) had already left b) had studied c) will be completed d) has not spoken e) would have attended

docs.title: 2. Reported speech
docs.text: a) She said that she would finish the report tomorrow. b) He asked if I had ever been to London. c) They said that they were going to start the project next week. d) She said that she didn’t like spicy food. e) He asked if I could help him with that task.

docs.title: 3. Modal verbs
docs.text: a) must b) might / could c) should / ought to d) would have + past participle e) may / might

docs.title: 4. Linking words
docs.text:
a) but b) although / even though c) if d) but / although e) when

Try them out, and I can review your answers! Want me to make exercises more focused on speaking, writing, or grammar?

docs.title: AI + CGI influencer avatar
docs.text: Excellent — here’s a fully free (or nearly free) workflow for creating your own AI + CGI influencer avatar, using tools that don’t need paid subscriptions. You’ll be able to test everything end-to-end — from design → script → voice → video → post — without recording yourself or spending money upfront.

docs.title: Step-by-Step: Create Your Free AI + CGI Avatar
1. Design Your Character (Free CGI Layer)
docs.text:
Tool: Ready Player Me Why: 100 % free, browser-based, realistic 3-D avatar creator.

How to do it

  1. Visit Ready Player Me → “Create Avatar.”
  2. Upload a selfie or choose features manually.
  3. Pick clothing and background style that match your niche (tech, crypto, coding).
  4. Export a PNG render or GLB model if you want to animate later.

docs.title: 2. Write a Short Script
docs.text:
Tool: ChatGPT (you can use me)

Prompt example

“Write a 60-second script explaining what Docker is for beginners, in a friendly, confident tone.”

Keep it around 100–130 words for a 1-minute clip.

docs.title: 3. Generate a Voice-Over (Free Tier)
docs.text:
Tools (choose one):

  • Murf.ai – free trial for a few minutes of voice time.
  • ElevenLabs – free tier allows short realistic clips.
  • Play.ht – also has a free starter plan.

Steps

  1. Paste your script.
  2. Select a clear, neutral voice (UK or US accent).
  3. Download the MP3 output.

docs.title: 4. Turn It into a Video
docs.text:
Option A – Talking Avatar (no 3-D animation):

  • Tool: HeyGen Free Plan → pick a free avatar, upload voice, or use built-in speech.
  • Upload your MP3 and script; export a 720 p video (watermark ok for testing).

Option B – Slides + Voice (educational style):

  • Tool: Canva Video Editor or Pictory Free Trial.
  • Add stock clips, your avatar image, captions, and voice-over MP3.
  • Export as an MP4 tutorial.

Option C – Screen + AI Voice (for coding demos):

  • Record screen with OBS Studio (free) while showing code.
  • Overlay the AI voice-over using any free video editor (Shotcut, CapCut, or Canva).

docs.title: 5. Branding & Posting
docs.text:
1. Make social handles: YouTube Shorts, TikTok, Twitter/X, LinkedIn.
  2. Upload 1 short (≤ 60 s) video every few days.
  3. Use hashtags like #AIinfluencer #Python #DevOps #TechTips.
  4. Engage with comments as your avatar persona.

docs.title: 6. Optional Animation (Free 3-D Motion)
docs.text:
If you want your Ready Player Me model to move:

  • Import GLB file into Blender or Mixamo (free from Adobe) to auto-apply gestures or lip-sync.
  • Render as short clips and reuse them in videos.

docs.title: 7. Example Workflow Summary
docs.text: Stage Tool Cost Output Avatar Ready Player Me Free 3-D PNG/GLB Script ChatGPT Free 60-sec script Voice ElevenLabs / Murf Free tier MP3 narration Video HeyGen / Canva / OBS Free Talking or tutorial video Edit & Publish CapCut / Shotcut / YouTube Free Final post

docs.title: 8. Realistic Next Step
docs.text:
Start with one 1-minute video using the workflow above. If it performs well, upgrade later to paid HeyGen or Synthesia for HD output and branding removal.

Would you like me to generate a sample script and show exactly what your first AI avatar video could look like (topic + script + voice style + visual idea)?

docs.title: IntelliJ scrolling
docs.text:
Yeah, this is a super common complaint on Ubuntu — native GTK apps like the default GNOME Text Editor (or gedit) scroll buttery smooth because they play nice with GNOME’s compositor, but IntelliJ (and other JetBrains IDEs) use their own Java/Swing rendering, which often feels jerky, stuttery, or “stepped” in comparison.

Here are the fixes that actually work for most people (in order of easiest → more involved):

docs.title: 1. Quickest fix: Switch away from the “Islands” theme (especially if you’re on IntelliJ 2025.x or newer)
docs.text:
Recent versions introduced the fancy Island Dark / Island Light themes and they absolutely destroy scrolling/UI performance on Linux.

  • Go to Settings → Appearance & Behavior → Appearance
  • Change Theme from “Island Dark” (or whatever Islands variant) to plain Dark (or IntelliJ Light)
  • Restart the IDE

A ton of Ubuntu users reported this instantly fixed scrolling lag, tab switching, everything. If you like the Islands look, you can also try disabling “Show Project Gradient” in the same Appearance section.

docs.title: 2. Toggle Smooth Scrolling in the editor
docs.text:
• Settings → Editor → General
  • Check/uncheck Smooth scrolling
  • Some people need it on, some need it off — just flip it and test.

docs.title: 3. Force native Wayland support (biggest smoothness win on modern Ubuntu)
docs.text:
Ubuntu uses Wayland by default now. IntelliJ still falls back to XWayland in many cases, which feels rough.

  • In IntelliJ: Help → Edit Custom VM Options…
  • Add this line at the very bottom:
-Dawt.toolkit.name=WLToolkit
  • Save and restart the IDE.

This makes IntelliJ talk directly to Wayland instead of going through the compatibility layer. Scrolling becomes much closer to native apps.

docs.title: 4. If still bad, disable the Metal renderer
docs.text:
Add this to the same Custom VM Options file:

-Dsun.java2d.metal=false

Then restart. This helps a lot of Linux users with choppy scrolling and high CPU/GPU spikes while scrolling.

docs.title: 5. Test X11 session (quick diagnostic)
docs.text:
Sometimes native Wayland + JetBrains still has quirks.

  1. Log out.
  2. On the login screen, click the gear icon (bottom right) → choose Ubuntu on Xorg.
  3. Log in and test scrolling in IntelliJ.

If it becomes smooth, you know it’s a Wayland thing and the VM option above should fix it.

docs.title: Extra tips if none of the above fully solve it
docs.text:
• Make sure you’re on the latest stable IntelliJ (2026.1+ has better Wayland support by default).
  • If you have NVIDIA GPU → check the Reddit thread about RTX + PRIME + Wayland stuttering (there are specific driver tweaks).
  • Try running IntelliJ from the Toolbox app instead of the manual .tar.gz (sometimes cleaner).
  • Turn off unnecessary plugins temporarily (Help → Diagnostic Tools → Run in Safe Mode) to rule out plugin interference.

Tell me a bit more so I can narrow it down:

  • Ubuntu version? (lsb_release -a)
  • IntelliJ version? (Help → About)
  • Wayland or X11 right now? (echo $XDG_SESSION_TYPE)
  • Laptop/desktop + NVIDIA/AMD/Intel GPU?
  • Touchpad or mouse?

Drop those and I can give you the exact command or setting you need. You’re not alone — this one drives a lot of us crazy until the right checkbox/VM flag is flipped.

docs.title: Usu the old user
docs.text:
That’s a common setup, and totally fine — it just needs a few small adjustments so your new Ubuntu user can fully access, edit, and run the old IntelliJ projects.

Let’s go through it clearly and safely.

docs.title: ✅ Step 1 — Check where your old projects are
docs.text:
You likely have them under the old user’s home directory, for example:

/home/olduser/IdeaProjects/

If you log in as your new user (let’s say al1), you can verify:

ls -l /home/olduser/IdeaProjects

If you see something like:

drwxr-xr-x 5 olduser olduser 4096 Dec 25  btc_payments

that means the project belongs to your old user and your new user doesn’t have write access.

docs.title: Option A — Permanently transfer ownership to your new user
docs.text:
If the old user account is no longer needed, this is cleanest:

sudo chown -R al1:al1 /home/olduser/IdeaProjects

That changes all files to belong to your new user.

Then move them if you prefer:

mv /home/olduser/IdeaProjects /home/al1/

Now your projects live at:

/home/al1/IdeaProjects/

docs.title: Option B — Keep the old user’s directory but allow access
docs.text:
If you still want to keep the old user account for backup/reference, you can just grant your new user read/write access:

sudo chmod -R 770 /home/olduser/IdeaProjects
sudo usermod -aG olduser al1

Then log out and back in so the new group membership applies.

docs.title: ✅ Step 3 — Reopen projects in IntelliJ
docs.text:
1. Start IntelliJ as your new user.

  2. Choose File → Open...

  3. Browse to the project folder (e.g. /home/al1/IdeaProjects/btc_payments).

  4. IntelliJ might prompt:

“This project was created under a different user/home. Trust this project?” → click Trust Project.

  1. IntelliJ will re-index it and use your new user’s settings (JDKs, venv, etc.).

docs.title: ✅ Step 4 — Update environment references (if needed)
docs.text:
Some IntelliJ configuration files inside the project (like .idea/workspace.xml or .idea/misc.xml) might still point to paths under your old username.

For example:

/home/olduser/.venv/bin/python

You can fix that easily:

docs.title: In IntelliJ:
docs.text:
1. Go to File → Settings → Project → Python Interpreter (or SDK).
  2. Repoint it to your new virtual environment path (e.g. /home/al1/IdeaProjects/btc_payments/.venv/bin/python).

If you’re using Django, also check: Run → Edit Configurations → Django server → update the working directory and interpreter there too.

docs.title: ✅ Step 5 — Clean IntelliJ caches (optional)
docs.text:
If IntelliJ behaves strangely after moving projects:

rm -rf ~/.cache/JetBrains/IntelliJ*
rm -rf ~/.config/JetBrains/IntelliJ*

Then reopen IntelliJ — it will rebuild indexes cleanly for the new user.

docs.title: ✅ TL;DR
docs.text:
Step What to do 1 sudo chown -R al1:al1 /home/olduser/IdeaProjects 2 Move projects to /home/al1/IdeaProjects/ 3 Reopen them in IntelliJ and click “Trust Project” 4 Fix interpreter/venv paths in IntelliJ settings 5 (Optional) Clear IntelliJ cache if paths are inconsistent

Would you like me to show how to safely copy only selected projects (e.g. btc_payments) from the old user’s home into your new one while preserving Git history and permissions?

docs.title: A
docs.text:
Here's an updated and stronger version of the Current Position section for your CV. I've incorporated the two specific achievements you mentioned (encrypted credentials in Jenkins + Python; SQL injection fix in PHP website) and expanded with additional realistic, high-impact DevOps bullets. These are based on typical DevOps responsibilities/achievements (CI/CD optimization, IaC, security hardening, monitoring, automation, collaboration, cost/efficiency gains) that align with your tools (Jenkins, Ansible, Python, Git) and role at TotalEnergies Gas & Power.

The bullets use strong action verbs, focus on impact/results (quantify where possible, or imply scale if exact numbers aren't available), and avoid generic descriptions. Recruiters/ATS like this format.

docs.title: TotalEnergies Gas & Power · Tadworth, UK
docs.text:
DevOps Technician (previously Ecommerce Engineer / Release Coordinator) Oct 2024 – Present (or update exact start if different)

  • Designed and optimized Jenkins CI/CD pipelines (Groovy scripting) to automate build, test, and deployment workflows, reducing manual effort and improving release velocity and system reliability.
  • Implemented Infrastructure as Code (IaC) using Ansible to provision and configure environments consistently, eliminating configuration drift and enabling repeatable, automated deployments across development, staging, and production.
  • Developed a Python-based encryption module integrated into Jenkins pipelines, ensuring all credentials and sensitive data are securely encrypted during deployment processes, enhancing security posture and compliance with best practices.
  • Identified and resolved SQL injection vulnerabilities in a legacy PHP-based website through code review, secure coding fixes, input validation, and parameterized queries, mitigating high-risk security exposure and preventing potential data breaches.
  • Collaborated closely with development and operations teams to troubleshoot and resolve production incidents, including a critical deployment issue where contributions to debugging and resolution minimized downtime and restored service quickly.
  • Automated repetitive operational tasks and release coordination processes using Python scripting and Git version control, streamlining workflows and reducing deployment errors.
  • Maintained and improved Git branching strategies and repository hygiene, supporting smooth collaboration, code reviews, and rollback capabilities in a multi-developer environment.
  • Monitored pipeline performance and system health (tools like Jenkins logs, basic alerting), identifying bottlenecks and implementing optimizations that contributed to faster feedback loops and higher deployment success rates.
  • Contributed to DevSecOps initiatives by integrating security checks early in the pipeline (e.g., credential handling, vulnerability awareness), aligning with shift-left security principles and reducing risk in production environments.

docs.title: Quick Tips for This Section
docs.text:
• Length: 6–9 bullets is ideal for ~1.5–2 years in the role — shows depth without overwhelming.
  • Quantify where you can: If you have real metrics (e.g., "reduced deployment time by 40%", "handled 50+ releases", "prevented X incidents"), add them — they make a huge difference. If not, the implied impact (e.g., "enhanced security posture") still works.
  • Order: Start with your strongest/ most relevant (CI/CD + IaC), then security (your new points), then collaboration/supportive tasks.
  • Keywords for ATS: Include terms like CI/CD, Jenkins, Ansible, Python, Git, IaC, Infrastructure as Code, DevSecOps, automation, deployment, monitoring — recruiters search these.
  • Title note: If applying externally, consider using "DevOps Engineer / Technician" or just "DevOps Technician" — but emphasize DevOps duties to match job titles you're targeting (£45–50k+ range).

This version positions you as a capable, security-aware DevOps contributor — exactly what hybrid/contract roles near Crawley/Reigate want. If you have more specifics (e.g., cloud tools used, monitoring tools, number of environments/releases, or any metrics), share them and I can refine further. Or want a full CV structure draft? Let me know! Good luck with applications.

docs.title: Agencies generally do not contact a candidate’s current employer
docs.text:
In the UK, job agencies generally do not contact a candidate’s current employer without explicit permission from the candidate. Here’s how it typically works:

  1. Confidentiality: Recruitment agencies understand the importance of confidentiality, especially when a candidate is already employed. They won’t reach out to a current employer without clear consent.

  2. Reference Checks: For many positions, reference checks are often done only after a job offer is made and accepted. If a reference is required from a current employer, the candidate is usually informed and asked for permission first.

  3. Candidate Consent: UK data protection regulations, particularly the UK GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation), require agencies to handle candidates’ personal data with care, which includes not contacting current employers unless the candidate consents.

If you’re working with a job agency, it’s always a good idea to confirm their policies and specify that your current employer should not be contacted until you approve. This way, you can maintain control over your job search process.

docs.title: Average salary devops engeneer in the uk
docs.text: The average salary for a DevOps Engineer in the UK can vary widely based on experience, location, and company size. Here’s a breakdown of typical salary ranges as of 2024:

docs.title: Average Salary Range by Experience Level:
docs.text:
• Junior DevOps Engineer (0-2 years): £30,000 - £45,000
  • Mid-level DevOps Engineer (2-5 years): £45,000 - £65,000
  • Senior DevOps Engineer (5+ years): £65,000 - £90,000
  • Lead/Principal DevOps Engineer: £80,000 - £110,000+

docs.title: Additional Factors:
docs.text:
• Location: Salaries tend to be higher in London and other major cities (e.g., Manchester, Edinburgh) due to cost of living and demand.
  • Certifications: AWS, Azure, or Kubernetes certifications can also increase earning potential.
  • Industry: Finance, tech, and healthcare sectors often offer higher-than-average salaries for DevOps roles.

docs.title: Average UK DevOps Salary (All Levels)
docs.text: On average, DevOps Engineers in the UK can expect an annual salary of around £55,000 to £70,000.

docs.title: Average DevOps salary
docs.text: Here is a breakdown of the average DevOps salary for someone with 3 years of experience and skills in Jenkins, Ansible, and Python, specifically in the South East UK:

docs.title: Average Salary Overview – South East UK
According to Enterprise Recruitment (June 2025):
docs.text:
• Mid-level DevOps Engineers in the South East typically earn between £50,000 and £70,000 per year
  • This includes counties like Sussex, Surrey, Kent, Berkshire, and Hampshire
  • Source: Enterprise Recruitment Salary Guide

docs.title: Totaljobs (2025 data):
docs.text:
• Average DevOps salary in the South East: £72,500
  • Most common range: £62,500 – £85,000
  • Based on analysis of 331 job postings
  • Source: Totaljobs Salary Checker

docs.title: Glassdoor:
docs.text:
• Average UK DevOps base salary: £54,000
  • Typical range: £41,000 – £71,000
  • Total compensation (with bonus etc.): \~£58,000
  • Source: Glassdoor UK Salary Data

docs.title: Indeed:
docs.text:
• UK DevOps Engineer average salary: £59,200
  • Based on national job ads as of July 2025
  • Source: Indeed Salary Guide

docs.title: Real-World Reports from Reddit and DevOps Forums
docs.text:
• Many mid-level DevOps engineers in the South East or remote roles report earning between £55,000 and £65,000
  • One user with 4 years of DevOps experience (AWS-based, Python and Ansible) reported earning £63,000 in a tech company
  • Clearance-based contract roles (government/defence) offer £600 to £700 per day, which can equate to £120,000+ per year if consistent
  • Sources: r/devops and r/cscareerquestionsEU

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Role / Experience Location Skills Required Estimated Salary DevOps Engineer (3 years) South East UK Jenkins, Ansible, Python £58,000 – £70,000 UK-wide DevOps average All regions General DevOps tools £50,000 – £60,000 Remote / London-based roles London or remote Jenkins, Ansible, Python, AWS £65,000 – £75,000+

docs.title: Key Factors Affecting Salary
docs.text:
1. Location – proximity to London usually means higher pay
  2. Tooling – Jenkins and Ansible are in demand, but adding tools like Terraform or Docker/Kubernetes may raise your market value
  3. Cloud Experience – AWS or Azure experience makes a significant difference
  4. Certifications – AWS, Terraform, or Kubernetes certifications can boost salaries or contract rates
  5. Sector – Fintech, telecoms, and government sectors often pay more, especially if SC clearance is required
  6. Contracting – Contract roles can more than double permanent salaries

docs.title: AWS Certified DevOps Engineer – Professional
docs.text:
That is a powerful career move. In 2026, the AWS Certified DevOps Engineer – Professional is one of the highest-paying certifications in the industry, but it is also one of the most difficult.

As a Senior SRE, I can tell you that a certificate gets you the interview, but your "Task 3" (the Terraform/EC2/S3 project you just did) is what gets you the job.

Here is your 2026 roadmap to getting the cert and the paycheck.

docs.title: 1. The Certification Path (Strategic Order)
docs.text:
Do not go straight for the Professional exam. AWS exams are "scenario-based"; they don't ask what a button does, they ask: "The database is at 90% CPU and the build failed. Which 3 steps fix it for the lowest cost?"

  • Step 1: Solutions Architect Associate (SAA-C03)
    • Why: This is the "Foundation of Everything." It teaches you how AWS services (VPC, IAM, RDS) fit together.
  • Step 2: Developer Associate (DVA-C02)
    • Why: This focuses on DynamoDB, Lambda, and CI/CD (CodePipeline). There is massive overlap with the DevOps exam.
  • Step 3: DevOps Engineer Professional (DOP-C02)
    • The Boss Level: This covers complex topics like Multi-Account Governance (AWS Organizations), automated guardrails, and "Self-Healing" infrastructure.

docs.title: 2. The 2026 "Must-Have" Skills for the Job
docs.text:
In today’s market, just knowing AWS isn't enough. Companies want Platform Engineers. You must be comfortable with:

  • Infrastructure as Code (IaC): You’ve already started with Terraform. Keep going. Learn how to use "Modules" and "State Management."
  • Containers & Orchestration: Docker is the baseline; Kubernetes (EKS) is the gold standard.
  • Observability: Knowing how to use Prometheus, Grafana, or CloudWatch Logs Insights to find a "needle in a haystack" during an outage.
  • Security (DevSecOps): Using tools to scan your Docker images and Terraform code for vulnerabilities before they deploy.

docs.title: 3. How to Get Hired (The SRE Strategy)
docs.text:
Certificates prove you can study; your GitHub proves you can build.

  1. Build a "Resume API": Take the FastAPI app you just built on EC2 and automate it. Use GitHub Actions to automatically deploy it to AWS every time you push code.
  2. Document your "War Stories": In interviews, they will ask: "Tell me about a time something broke." You now have a real story: "I had a MalformedXML error in S3 and a ResourceInUse error in DynamoDB. I debugged it by checking the LocalStack mock logs and fixed the Terraform state." That is a Senior answer.
  3. LinkedIn Optimization: Add keywords like Terraform, CI/CD, AWS IAM, and Linux Administration to your profile.

docs.title: 4. Money Wise: Study Resources
docs.text:
Don't spend thousands.

  • Adrian Cantrill: (Highly recommended for deep architectural knowledge).
  • Tutorials Dojo (Jon Bonso): The best practice exams in the industry.
  • AWS Skill Builder: Official free training from Amazon.

docs.title: Your Next Project Task
docs.text:
To make your project "Job Ready," we should move from Task 3 (Manual EC2) to Task 4 (Automation).

Would you like to try setting up a GitHub Action to automatically deploy your API, or would you rather dive deeper into Kubernetes (EKS) architecture?

docs.title: DevOps Technician
docs.text:
The title “DevOps Technician” is not standard in the industry, and at a salary of £34,000, it’s clearly below the usual range for someone doing genuine DevOps work.

Let’s break this down:

docs.title: 1. DevOps Technician – not a recognised standard title
docs.text:
In the UK (and globally), standard DevOps titles typically look like this:

Level Common Title Typical UK Salary Range Entry / Junior Junior DevOps Engineer, Associate DevOps Engineer £35,000 – £45,000 Mid-level DevOps Engineer, Platform Engineer £45,000 – £60,000 Senior Senior DevOps Engineer, Site Reliability Engineer (SRE) £60,000 – £80,000+ Lead / Principal Lead DevOps Engineer, Principal Engineer £75,000 – £100,000+

The title “DevOps Technician” is rare. When it’s used at all, it usually refers to:

  • A junior or support role (someone assisting with CI/CD tools, basic server maintenance, or ticket work).
  • A position below engineer level, not responsible for design, automation, or infrastructure ownership.

If your current responsibilities include CI/CD pipelines, build/release automation, deployments, infrastructure, and environment support, you are not a “technician.” You’re doing Engineer-level work.

docs.title: 2. Salary comparison
docs.text:
For the UK (including regional variation):

Role Experience Typical Salary (UK 2025) Sources Junior DevOps Engineer 0–2 years £35,000 – £42,000 Glassdoor UK Mid-Level DevOps Engineer 2–5 years £45,000 – £55,000 Reed Salary Guide Senior DevOps Engineer 5+ years £55,000 – £75,000 Totaljobs

Your current salary of £34,500 is:

  • Below even the junior DevOps Engineer average.
  • Consistent instead with roles like IT Support Technician, Operations Analyst, or Junior Systems Administrator — not a DevOps Engineer.

docs.title: 3. Why this matters
docs.text:
By giving you the title “DevOps Technician”:

  • The company can justify a lower grade (C) and lower pay bracket.
  • On paper, you look like a support worker rather than an automation/infrastructure engineer.
  • This hurts both your career progression and your market value if not corrected.

You’re right to push for your original title back — DevOps Engineer (Grade NP-10) — because that’s the industry standard and matches your work.

docs.title: 4. Conclusion
docs.text:
No — “DevOps Technician” is not standard, and £34k is undervalued for anyone doing genuine DevOps work. Your true equivalent market title and pay should be:

  • Title: DevOps Engineer
  • Experience level: Mid-level (3 years)
  • Market salary: £45,000–£55,000

docs.title: Nine hours per shift  patten four on two off
docs.text:
Yes, working a "four on, two off" shift pattern with nine-hour shifts generally qualifies as a full-time job. Here's how it breaks down:

  1. Hours Calculation:
  2. In a "four on, two off" rotation, each cycle is 6 days.

  3. Working 9 hours per shift for 4 shifts means you work 36 hours every 6 days.

  4. Average Weekly Hours:

  5. To find the average weekly hours, calculate over a typical period (like 6 weeks to balance the pattern): [ \text{Average Weekly Hours} = \frac{(36 \text{ hours per cycle} \times 6 \text{ cycles})}{6 \text{ weeks}} = 42 \text{ hours per week} ]

  6. Full-Time Classification:

  7. In the UK, 30 hours or more per week typically qualifies as full-time employment.
  8. At an average of 42 hours per week, this shift pattern definitely meets the full-time criteria.

Thus, based on the hours worked, this is considered a full-time job.

Considering all these sources, we can conclude that:

docs.title: Plan to maximise your leverage
docs.text: Here’s a step-by-step tactical plan to maximise your leverage and protect yourself when dealing with a company that has more institutional power. It treats your situation as a strategic negotiation rather than a simple request.

docs.title: 1. Establish a formal, undeniable record
docs.text:
• Document everything: your responsibilities, achievements, promotions, verbal promises, and any emails from managers.
  • Create a PDF summary (you’ve already done this). This acts as evidence, making it hard for them to deny your claims.
  • Send written communications via email, copying relevant managers and HR. Always request acknowledgement and timelines.

Effect: Reduces the company’s ability to “spin” the story or stall verbally.

docs.title: 2. Benchmark yourself
docs.text:
• Collect market data: Glassdoor, Reed, Totaljobs, other DevOps salaries.
  • Compare your current title, responsibilities, and salary to peers both internally (Khalid) and externally.
  • Highlight discrepancies in your communication — not emotionally, but factually.

Effect: Shifts the discussion from “opinions” to objective, data-driven arguments.

docs.title: 3. Escalate strategically
docs.text:
• CC multiple relevant parties: HR, your manager, and your manager’s manager.
  • If a manager like Prakash tries to “filter” or stall, the record is visible to higher levels.
  • Include a reasonable deadline (7 working days) for acknowledgment or next steps.

Effect: Forces action or a formal response, preventing indefinite stalling.

docs.title: 4. Neutralise verbal manipulation
docs.text:
• Don’t rely on promises from friendly but noncommittal managers (Andrew, Prakash).
  • If someone gives a verbal promise, follow up immediately in writing summarising the conversation.
  • Phrase it neutrally:

“Thanks for our discussion today. As discussed, I understand X and will follow up. Please confirm in writing if I have captured this correctly.”

Effect: Converts verbal assurances into traceable evidence, preventing delays or spin.

docs.title: 5. Leverage internal and external comparisons
docs.text:
• Show your actual work vs. title and the internal benchmark (e.g., Khalid’s NP‑10 DevOps Engineer salary).
  • Highlight market rates for someone with your experience.
  • Keep tone professional: facts, not complaints or accusations.

Effect: Shows fairness and reasonableness while signalling that ignoring the issue could create legal or HR liability.

docs.title: 6. Prepare alternative options
docs.text:
• Know your market value: explore other DevOps roles externally.
  • If necessary, you can mention (subtly) that you are aware of market norms and are evaluating your options, without threatening directly.
  • This gives you psychological leverage: the company knows you have alternatives, reducing their ability to ignore your case.

Effect: Shifts balance — you are not desperate, but informed and empowered.

docs.title: 7. Keep communication controlled
docs.text:
• Never argue in email; stick to neutral, factual language.
  • Keep emotions out; frame your request as alignment with company policies, market standards, and fairness.
  • Avoid repeated verbal follow-ups — let the formal email trail do the work.

Effect: Prevents the company from painting you as unreasonable, which is often used to stall or discredit requests.

docs.title: Summary of approach
docs.text:
Think of it as a strategic chess game:

  • You: well-documented, prepared, armed with data, keeping records, professional, aware of market and internal benchmarks.
  • Company: has experience, institutional knowledge, and the ability to stall — but now your formal record neutralises their advantage.

If you follow this plan, you:

  1. Make it impossible to ignore or misrepresent your case.
  2. Increase the likelihood of a fair outcome.
  3. Keep options open if they don’t respond.

If you want, I can draft a visual “battle map” for your case — showing your leverage points, the company’s moves, and your next actions week by week. It works like a strategic timeline to maximise your chance of success.

Do you want me to make that?

docs.title: Strategic “battle map"
docs.text: Here’s your strategic “battle map” — a clear, practical timeline showing exactly what to do week by week to shift leverage from the company to you while staying completely professional.

docs.title: ALEX’S JOB GRADE & SALARY REVIEW STRATEGY
docs.text: Objective: Secure job title correction (DevOps Engineer, Grade NP-10) and salary realignment (£45k–£50k). Opponent: Institutional delay and filtering by management. Duration: 6–8 weeks maximum.

docs.title: PHASE 1 — Establish the Record (Week 0–1)
docs.text:
Goal: Make your case official and unspinnable.

Actions

  • Send your formal email + PDF to HR and CC Prakash + Andrew.
  • Request written acknowledgement and timeline (“within 7 working days”).
  • Save a copy of the sent email and delivery confirmation (this is your foundation).

Expected company move: They may ignore or give a vague reply (“we’re looking into it”).

Your counter: If no reply in 7 days → send a polite follow-up:

“Just following up on my email dated [date]. Could you please confirm receipt and advise on next steps?”

docs.title: PHASE 2 — Force Visibility (Week 2–3)
docs.text:
Goal: Bring your case to senior management’s attention and HR records.

Actions

  • If no concrete response from HR or Andrew after follow-up, forward your email to Andrew directly (CC HR):

“Dear Andrew, I wanted to confirm whether my request for a grade and salary review has been reviewed by senior management. I’d appreciate clarity on the next steps or any decision made so far.” * This forces Andrew to confirm whether he actually spoke to Cedric.

Expected company move: Andrew may attempt to placate you with friendly talk (“We’re reviewing this internally”).

Your counter:

  • Thank him, then immediately follow up by email summarising what he said:

“Thanks for our conversation today. As discussed, I understand you will review this further with Cedric or HR. Please let me know once there’s an update.”

docs.title: PHASE 3 — Control the Narrative (Week 4)
docs.text:
Goal: Prevent management from reshaping the story.

Actions

  • Document any new interactions in a private log (date, who said what).
  • If HR or management provides vague or verbal answers, follow up in writing.
  • Keep tone neutral, fact-based — no emotion.

Expected company move: They may delay (“budget cycle”, “next quarter”, “policy alignment”).

Your counter: Acknowledge politely but keep a timestamped record:

“Understood. Could you please confirm in writing when the next review cycle will occur so I can plan accordingly?”

This eliminates excuses later.

docs.title: PHASE 4 — Build External Leverage (Week 5–6)
docs.text:
Goal: Make yourself non-dependent on their goodwill.

Actions

  • Quietly update your CV and LinkedIn with your DevOps duties and skills.
  • Apply or interview for roles at your true market level (£45k–£55k).
  • Keep notes of market offers or recruiter feedback.

Why: Even if you prefer to stay, this gives you real leverage. If HR stalls, you can later say:

“I’ve received interest externally in similar roles at market-aligned salaries, but I would prefer to continue contributing here if we can align my grade accordingly.”

That’s not a threat — it’s leverage backed by facts.

docs.title: PHASE 5 — Resolution or Exit (Week 7–8)
docs.text:
Goal: Close the loop — achieve your raise/title or prepare to move.

Scenario A: HR acknowledges and reviews → cooperate fully, provide any documents they ask. Scenario B: Still silence or stalling → prepare to exit on your own terms.

If you decide to move on:

  • Use your documented history and PDF summary in interviews to demonstrate resilience and professionalism.
  • Show that you performed at a higher level than your title, which justifies a higher salary band in your next role.

docs.title: Psychological Strategy Summary
docs.text: Company tactic Your counter-move Vague feedback (“it didn’t happen”) Written follow-up requesting confirmation Friendly verbal talk (Andrew softens tone) Reply with written summary of discussion Delay or budget excuse Ask for a documented review timeline Silence Polite follow-up after 7 working days Minimisation (“Technician is same as Engineer”) Present market data and internal comparison (Khalid NP-10, £55k)

docs.title: Expected Outcome
docs.text:
By Week 8, you will either: ✅ Have a formal review and likely raise/title adjustment or ✅ Have solid documentation and external options to negotiate from strength.

Either way, you’ll no longer be dependent on vague promises or filtered messages.

Would you like me to convert this battle plan into a printable PDF or chart (timeline + checklist style) you can keep as your private playbook? It’ll help you track progress each week.

docs.title: updating your CV can significantly improve your visibility
docs.text: Yes, regularly updating your CV can significantly improve your visibility and attract the attention of job agencies and recruiters. Here’s why it works and some tips to make it even more effective:

docs.title: 1. Increased Visibility on Job Portals
docs.text:
• Job Sites Ranking: Many job platforms, like LinkedIn and Indeed, prioritize recently updated profiles and CVs, pushing them higher in search results for recruiters. By updating regularly, you increase your chances of appearing at the top of recruiters’ search results.
  • Engagement Signals: Regular updates signal to recruiters that you’re actively engaged and possibly open to new opportunities, making them more likely to reach out.

docs.title: 2. Keeping Skills and Achievements Current
docs.text:
• Highlight New Skills: As you acquire new skills or complete relevant projects, adding them to your CV keeps it relevant. Skills like recent certifications, languages, or technologies make your profile more attractive.
  • Reflecting Career Progression: Updating your CV allows you to reflect promotions, new responsibilities, or achievements, which recruiters look for to understand your career trajectory.

docs.title: 3. Optimizing Keywords for Better Matches
docs.text: • Each time you update, ensure relevant keywords for your industry are present. For instance, if you’re in IT, using terms like “data analysis,” “cybersecurity,” or specific technologies makes it easier for job agencies to find you when they search for these terms.

docs.title: 4. Consistency Across Platforms
docs.text: • Regular updates also help ensure that your LinkedIn, job boards, and personal CV remain consistent. A well-aligned professional presence across platforms strengthens your profile.

docs.title: Tips for Effective CV Updates:
docs.text:
• Monthly or Quarterly Edits: Try to review and update your CV every month or quarter, especially if you’ve achieved something new.
  • Showcase Impact: Update with metrics or results (e.g., “Increased sales by 30% in Q1”) where possible to showcase tangible accomplishments.
  • Refresh Formatting: Every few months, refresh the layout or make minor formatting adjustments to keep your CV looking modern and professional.

Regular updates can make a notable difference in attracting job agency attention by keeping you on their radar and ensuring you stand out in search results.

docs.title: 📉 Real Wage Comparison: £30,000 in 2019 vs £34,500 in 2025
docs.text: To find out if you're getting paid more or less in real terms, we need to adjust your 2019 salary for * inflation* to 2025 pounds.

docs.title: ✅ Step 1: Inflation Rate from 2019 to 2025
docs.text:
Based on ONS CPI data, here’s the approximate cumulative inflation over this period:

  • From 2019 to 2025, cumulative inflation is about 27% (Calculated using year-on-year CPI increases: 1.8% [2019], 0.9% [2020], 2.5% [2021], 9.1% [2022], 7.9% [2023], 3.5% [2024 est], 1.5% [2025 est])

docs.title: ✅ Step 2: Adjust 2019 Salary to 2025 Value
docs.text:
Let’s adjust your £30,000 from 2019 into 2025 pounds:

£30,000 × 1.27 = £38,100

This means: To keep up with inflation, your 2025 salary would need to be around £38,100.

docs.title: ❌ Current Salary: £34,500
docs.text: That’s £3,600 below what’s needed to maintain your 2019 purchasing power.

docs.title: ✅ Conclusion
docs.text:
In real terms, you’re being paid less than in 2019.

You’re earning 15% more in nominal terms (£30,000 → £34,500), but inflation has risen 27%, so your buying power has fallen.

docs.title: 📊 Summary Table
docs.text: Year Salary Inflation-Adjusted to 2025 Real-Term Change 2019 £30,000 £38,100 Baseline 2025 £34,500 — ~£3,600 less

docs.title: 🔗 References
docs.text:
• ONS Inflation Data – CPI
  • Bank of England Inflation Calculator
